case_studies.name: lock_free_queue_x86_to_arm
case_studies.description: Lock-free SPSC queue using store-release/load-acquire, ported from x86 to ARM
case_studies.context: A producer stores data then sets a flag. Consumer reads flag then data. On x86 TSO, the store ordering is guaranteed. On ARM, without dmb, the consumer may see the flag before the data.

case_studies.name: dekker_mutex_arm
case_studies.description: Dekker's mutual exclusion algorithm on ARM
case_studies.context: Dekker's algorithm relies on store-buffering visibility. On x86, only SB reordering breaks it. On ARM, all reorderings are possible, requiring full barriers.

case_studies.name: seqlock_reader_riscv
case_studies.description: Seqlock reader pattern ported to RISC-V
case_studies.context: Seqlock readers observe a sequence number then read data. The MP pattern captures the key ordering requirement. RISC-V RVWMO may reorder both stores and loads.

case_studies.name: cuda_kernel_wg_scope
case_studies.description: CUDA kernel with workgroup-scoped synchronization across CTAs
case_studies.context: A CUDA kernel uses __threadfence_block() (CTA scope) but threads communicate across CTAs. This is a scope mismatch: CTA-scoped fences don't order cross-CTA communication.

case_studies.name: iriw_multicore_arm
case_studies.description: IRIW pattern in multi-core ARM system
case_studies.context: Two independent writers and two readers. On x86 (multi-copy atomic), both readers must agree on write order. On ARM (not multi-copy atomic), readers may disagree. This is observable on real Cortex-A53/A57 hardware.

case_studies.name: rcu_publish_riscv
case_studies.context: Linux RCU uses rcu_assign_pointer (store with release semantics) and rcu_dereference (load with consume/address dependency). The MP pattern captures this, and RISC-V RVWMO requires explicit fences.

case_studies.name: opencl_gpu_barrier_scope
case_studies.description: OpenCL kernel barrier with wrong scope across workgroups
case_studies.fence_recommendation: use device-scope barrier (work_group_barrier insufficient for cross-workgroup)
case_studies.context: An OpenCL kernel uses barrier(CLK_LOCAL_MEM_FENCE) but accesses global memory across workgroups. The workgroup-scoped barrier does not synchronize global memory across workgroups.

case_studies.name: asymmetric_fence_riscv
case_studies.description: RISC-V asymmetric fence (fence w,r) insufficient for MP
case_studies.context: ARM's dmb covers all orderings symmetrically. RISC-V fence w,r only orders stores-before-reads. For the MP pattern, the reader thread needs fence r,r to order its loads, but fence w,r on the reader only orders writes-before-reads, missing the load-load ordering.

llm_generated.name: Lock-Free Queue Data Race
llm_generated.description: A lock-free queue implementation relies on relaxed memory ordering, assuming atomicity of certain instructions. On ARM, reordering due to weaker memory barriers can cause the consumer to observe inconsistent state, leading to data corruption.

llm_generated.name: Lazy Initialization Race
llm_generated.description: A kernel module lazily initializes a shared resource without proper memory barriers, assuming initialization is complete once a flag is set. RISC-V's weaker memory model may cause other threads to see the flag as set but still observe uninitialized data, resulting in undefined behavior.

llm_generated.name: Concurrent Hash Map Update
llm_generated.description: A database engine performs concurrent updates to a hash map without sufficient synchronization, expecting updates to be visible immediately. On ARM, reordering can cause lookup threads to see stale pointers, leading to incorrect data retrieval or crashes.

llm_generated.name: Reader-Writer Lock Violation
llm_generated.description: A lock-free reader-writer synchronization is used with relaxed memory operations, assuming that writers' updates are visible to readers. RISC-V's weaker consistency can result in readers observing partial or stale updates, violating correctness guarantees.

llm_generated.name: Double-Checked Locking Flaw
llm_generated.description: A singleton object is initialized using double-checked locking without proper memory barriers, expecting the initialized object to be safe after the first check. On ARM, instruction reordering may cause multiple threads to see the object as initialized while it is not fully constructed, leading to subtle bugs.
